narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 41, which stands out above the typical student's score of around 37. However, the admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 40% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. The most influential factor pulling against you was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, well below the average of about 5.5, which strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar failure compared to peers who ranked higher. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also below the typical 5.7, added to that concern, reinforcing a pattern of mid-pack performance in your school's rankings. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, lower than the average 3.2, nudged the prediction slightly toward failure, though less forcefully. On a brighter note, your LSAT score was the single biggest positive force, pushing the model toward expecting you to pass, much like it does for top scorers. Your middle family income bracket played a small role in raising concerns, while planning to attend full-time—shared by over 90% of students—helped a bit by aligning with successful paths. Overall, your academic deciles stood out as weaker than most applicants who get approved, painting a picture riskier than the typical student's stronger school rankings and GPAs. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes bar passage stats. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps targeting a higher undergrad-level GPA equivalent, and leverage that impressive LSAT by applying to schools with holistic reviews. You might also seek bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself to build a stronger case next time. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
